evals.prompt: We're scoping a TPRM programme. We're a payments fintech headquartered in Ireland, operating across the EU and with a UK entity. We process cardholder and personal data, we rely heavily on AWS and a couple of offshore dev shops in India, and we've just embedded a third-party AI fraud-scoring model. We think we need Cybersecurity, Data Privacy and Financial Risk in scope. Review that and tell us what regulations apply and whether our domain selection is right.
evals.expected_output: A scoping review that maps jurisdictions/sector to obligations (DORA as EU financial entity, EBA/PSD2, GDPR + UK GDPR with SCCs/IDTA for India + AWS transfers, EU AI Act for the fraud model, NIS2 possibly) and flags GAPS in the domain selection: Operational Resilience (DORA), Fourth-Party (India dev shops, AWS sub-processors), Concentration (AWS dependency), AI/Model Risk (the AI model), arguably ABAC. Should explain why each applies and recommend adding the missing domains.
assertions: Identifies DORA as applicable (EU financial entity) and explains its third-party requirements
Identifies GDPR/UK GDPR and the need for a cross-border transfer mechanism for India and US/AWS
Identifies the EU AI Act as applicable to the third-party fraud-scoring model
Flags Operational Resilience as a gap (not selected but required under DORA)
Flags Fourth-Party / sub-processor risk as a gap given offshore dev shops and AWS
Flags AI / Model Risk as a gap given the embedded third-party model
Distinguishes mandatory (regulatory) obligations from recommended ones

evals.prompt: Here's our TPRM engagement configuration — review it for gaps, conflicts and over-scoping before we sign off. Domains: Cybersecurity, Data Privacy, ESG, Physical Security. Jurisdictions: UK only, we're a 400-person SaaS company, turnover about GBP 50m, no regulated activities. Lifecycle phases: Intake, Due Diligence, Continuous Monitoring, Offboarding. Deliverables we want: Due Diligence Questionnaires, Security Clauses, KPI/KRI Catalogue, Executive Dashboard. We do NOT want a TPRM Policy or Operating Model yet.
evals.expected_output: A coherence review that surfaces: GAP — no Risk Scoring/Tiering despite Due Diligence and monitoring needing it, and no Risk Tiering phase; GAP — Privacy Addendum/DPA missing though Data Privacy in scope and UK GDPR applies; GAP — Modern Slavery Act applies (>GBP 36m UK turnover) supporting ESG/supply-chain, but supply-chain due diligence deliverable missing; OVER-SCOPE — Physical Security for a SaaS vendor population likely lacks a driver, confirm; DEPENDENCY/CONFLICT — declining Policy/Operating Model while requesting SOPs-adjacent operational deliverables and dashboards undermines governance foundation; DEPENDENCY — KPI/KRI Catalogue must precede Executive Dashboard. Should be proportionate (not over-engineered for a non-regulated SME).
assertions: Flags the absence of a Risk Scoring Methodology / tiering as a gap given Due Diligence and monitoring are in scope
Flags missing Privacy Addendum/DPA despite Data Privacy domain and UK GDPR applicability
Identifies Modern Slavery Act applicability (UK turnover > GBP 36m) relevant to ESG/supply chain
Flags Physical Security as a likely over-scope item for a SaaS vendor population and asks for rationale
Notes the dependency that KPI/KRI Catalogue precedes the Executive Dashboard
Raises the risk of declining a TPRM Policy/Operating Model while requesting operational and reporting deliverables
Keeps recommendations proportionate to a non-regulated 400-person SME

evals.prompt: We operate in the US (including a New York banking entity), Germany, and Singapore. We're a financial services group. Before we pick domains and deliverables, just give me the regulatory obligation map — what third-party/outsourcing and related rules apply in each location and what they'll demand of our TPRM programme.
evals.expected_output: A jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction regulatory obligation map: US federal FFIEC/OCC interagency third-party guidance + GLBA Safeguards; New York specifically DFS 23 NYCRR 500 (third-party service provider security, MFA, encryption); Germany/EU DORA + EBA outsourcing guidelines + GDPR + possibly CSRD/LkSG/CSDDD for ESG supply chain; Singapore MAS Outsourcing and TRM Guidelines. Each should state what it requires of TPRM (registers, clauses, due diligence, exit plans, notification). Should note extraterritorial/cross-cutting items and where legal review is needed.
assertions: Identifies US FFIEC/OCC interagency third-party risk guidance and GLBA for the US financial entity
Specifically identifies NY DFS 23 NYCRR 500 for the New York banking entity
Identifies DORA and EBA outsourcing guidelines for the German/EU financial operations
Identifies MAS outsourcing / technology risk management guidelines for Singapore
States what each regime requires of a TPRM programme (e.g., register, contract clauses, exit, notification)
Organises the output clearly by jurisdiction and notes where local legal review is advisable

evals.prompt: Produce a full TPRM scoping and configuration report for this client: a UK NHS-adjacent healthcare provider, 2,000 staff, handles patient health records, uses several SaaS clinical systems and an offshore medical transcription service in the Philippines. They want a complete net-new TPRM programme — all lifecycle phases, full governance, assessment, operational, contract, reporting deliverables. Current state is a basic spreadsheet. Give me the scoping report.
evals.expected_output: A complete scoping report following the template: engagement context (healthcare, UK, special-category health data, offshore processor, low maturity net-new), domain scope (Cyber, Privacy mandatory, Operational Resilience, Fourth-Party for offshore, plus others with rationale), regulatory map (UK GDPR special-category Art.9, DPA 2018, NHS DSPT, Caldicott, cross-border transfer to Philippines via IDTA, Modern Slavery), lifecycle phases all in scope, coherence findings, deliverables plan with sequencing, recommended configuration, open questions, and a phased roadmap appropriate to low maturity. Should sequence governance before operations and note quick wins.
assertions: Follows the scoping report structure (context, domain scope, regulatory map, phases, findings, deliverables plan, roadmap)
Identifies UK GDPR special-category (health) data obligations and NHS DSPT for healthcare suppliers
Addresses the cross-border transfer to the Philippines (UK IDTA / transfer mechanism)
Flags Fourth-Party / offshore processor risk for the transcription service
Produces a phased roadmap that sequences governance foundation before operational deliverables
Tailors the roadmap to a low-maturity, net-new programme and identifies quick wins
Includes a coherence findings section and open questions for the client

evals.prompt: Our procurement lead drafted this deliverables wishlist and I want to know if the order makes sense and if anything's missing: they want us to start by writing all the SOPs and the RACI, then do a Tool Capability Assessment, then build the KPI dashboard, then later figure out the risk scoring methodology and write the TPRM policy at the end. We're an energy company, EU + UK operations, moderate maturity. Sanity-check the sequencing.
evals.expected_output: A dependency/sequencing critique: SOPs and RACI require the Operating Model first (and Policy is foundational, not last) — writing SOPs before the governance foundation is backwards; risk scoring methodology must precede assessment templates and should come early, not late; the KPI dashboard requires a KPI/KRI catalogue first; Tool Capability Assessment should follow workflow/requirements definition. Should propose a corrected build order (foundation -> governance -> methodology -> operations -> reporting -> technology) and note NIS2/energy sector and EU+UK obligations that may add supply-chain/cyber scope.
assertions: Identifies that TPRM Policy is foundational and should not be sequenced last
Identifies that SOPs and RACI depend on the Operating Model being defined first
Identifies that the Risk Scoring Methodology must precede assessment templates and should come early
Identifies that the KPI/KRI Catalogue must precede the Executive Dashboard
Proposes a corrected, dependency-respecting build order
Notes energy-sector / NIS2 and EU+UK considerations relevant to scope
